name: cloudflare
context: Cloudflare platform — Workers edge compute, KV, R2 storage, D1 database, Pages, DNS, and Wrangler CLI
info: The Cloudflare ecosystem covers edge-first application deployment and infrastructure management. Workers run JavaScript/TypeScript/Wasm at the edge in V8 isolates with sub-millisecond cold starts. KV provides globally distributed key-value storage. R2 offers S3-compatible object storage with zero egress fees. D1 is serverless SQLite for relational data. Pages deploys static and full-stack applications with automatic Git integration. The Wrangler CLI is the primary development and deployment interface. The Cloudflare REST API (v4) at api.cloudflare.com provides programmatic access to all services. Agents use this ecosystem to deploy applications, manage DNS, store data, and orchestrate edge infrastructure.
body:
# Cloudflare Ecosystem

Cloudflare is an edge-first platform where agents deploy applications, manage DNS, store data, and run compute globally in 300+ data centers. Unlike container-based serverless (AWS Lambda, Vercel Functions), Workers use V8 isolates — the same engine as Chrome — with sub-millisecond cold starts and no container overhead.

## Architecture

Cloudflare's developer platform is built around Workers as the compute primitive. Every other service — KV, R2, D1, Queues, Durable Objects — connects to Workers through **bindings**, which are zero-latency references configured in `wrangler.toml`.

```
Request → Cloudflare Edge (300+ PoPs)
            ↓
        Worker (V8 isolate)
            ↓
    ┌───────┼───────┐
    KV      R2      D1
  (k/v)  (objects) (SQL)
```

## Services

- **[[cloudflare/workers]]** — Edge compute: JavaScript/TypeScript/Wasm functions running in V8 isolates
- **[[cloudflare/kv]]** — Workers KV: globally distributed, eventually consistent key-value storage
- **[[cloudflare/r2]]** — R2: S3-compatible object storage with zero egress fees
- **[[cloudflare/d1]]** — D1: serverless SQLite database with automatic replication
- **[[cloudflare/pages]]** — Pages: deploy static and full-stack applications from Git
- **[[cloudflare/dns]]** — DNS: authoritative DNS management with sub-10ms global resolution
- **[[cloudflare/wrangler]]** — Wrangler CLI: develop, test, and deploy locally and to production

## Cross-Cutting Concerns

- **[[cloudflare/auth]]** — API tokens, Global API Key, and permissions for API access
- **[[cloudflare/troubleshooting]]** — Common errors, debugging, limits, and observability

## Environment Variables

| Variable | Purpose |
|----------|----------|
| `CLOUDFLARE_API_TOKEN` | API token with scoped permissions (preferred) |
| `CLOUDFLARE_ACCOUNT_ID` | Account identifier for API calls |
| `CLOUDFLARE_API_KEY` | Legacy Global API Key (use API tokens instead) |
| `CLOUDFLARE_EMAIL` | Account email (only with Global API Key) |

## API

All Cloudflare services are accessible via the REST API v4 at `https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/`. Authentication is via Bearer token in the `Authorization` header. Responses follow a standard envelope:

```json
{
  "success": true,
  "errors": [],
  "messages": [],
  "result": { ... }
}
```

## Libraries

- **wrangler** — Official CLI (`npm install -g wrangler`)
- **cloudflare** — Official Node.js SDK (`npm install cloudflare`)
- **@cloudflare/workers-types** — TypeScript types for Workers runtime APIs
- **miniflare** — Local Workers simulator (bundled with Wrangler)
- **python-cloudflare** — Python SDK for the Cloudflare API

## Agent Jobs

The primary tasks an agent performs in this ecosystem:

- **Deploy a Worker** — Write and deploy edge functions. See [[cloudflare/workers]].
- **Store key-value data** — Read/write to globally distributed KV. See [[cloudflare/kv]].
- **Upload files** — Store and serve objects via R2. See [[cloudflare/r2]].
- **Query a database** — Run SQL against D1 serverless SQLite. See [[cloudflare/d1]].
- **Deploy a site** — Ship static or full-stack apps via Pages. See [[cloudflare/pages]].
- **Manage DNS** — Add, update, delete DNS records for domains. See [[cloudflare/dns]].

sections.title: Platform architecture
sections.body: Cloudflare's developer platform uses Workers as the compute primitive. Workers run in V8 isolates at the edge — not containers, not VMs. Every storage service (KV, R2, D1) connects to Workers through bindings configured in `wrangler.toml`. Bindings are zero-latency references injected into the Worker's `env` object at runtime. This architecture means compute and data co-locate at the edge, minimizing latency. See [[cloudflare/workers#bindings]] for binding configuration.

sections.title: API overview
sections.body: The Cloudflare REST API v4 (`https://api.cloudflare.com/client/v4/`) provides programmatic access to all services. Authenticate with a Bearer token via the `Authorization` header. All responses use a standard envelope with `success`, `errors`, `messages`, and `result` fields. Pagination uses `page` and `per_page` query parameters. Rate limits vary by endpoint but are generally 1,200 requests per 5 minutes. See [[cloudflare/auth]] for token setup.

sections.title: wrangler.toml configuration
sections.body: Every Cloudflare project uses `wrangler.toml` as the central configuration file. It defines the Worker name, entry point, compatibility date, account ID, and all service bindings. Key fields: `name` (worker name), `main` (entry file), `compatibility_date` (runtime version), `account_id`, and binding blocks like `[[kv_namespaces]]`, `[[r2_buckets]]`, `[[d1_databases]]`. See [[cloudflare/wrangler]] for full CLI usage.

sections.title: Pricing model
sections.body: Workers Free plan: 100,000 requests/day, 10ms CPU time. Workers Paid ($5/mo): 10 million requests/month included, then $0.50/million. KV: 100k reads/day free, $0.50/million reads paid. R2: 10GB storage free, zero egress always. D1: 5 million rows read/day free. Pages: unlimited sites and requests on free plan. Most agents operate well within free tier limits for development and small production workloads.

related.note: Workers are the compute primitive for the entire platform
API tokens are required for all programmatic access
Wrangler is the primary development and deployment interface
Vercel is an alternative deployment platform, less edge-native
Lambda is container-based serverless, Workers are V8 isolate-based
S3 is the alternative to R2, but R2 has zero egress fees
